steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: EdgeKV is a tiny notebook in every Akamai office. Receptionists (EdgeWorkers) read and write to it instantly — no calling head office for every lookup.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
EdgeKV is a low-latency, eventually-consistent KV store accessible from EdgeWorkers. Reads happen at the edge in single-digit ms; writes propagate globally in seconds.

- Namespaces + groups for organization
- Per-item TTL
- Tokens scoped per namespace
- Designed for read-heavy edge state

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Common patterns: feature flags, A/B test assignments, valet-claim-check token storage, geo-IP overrides, kill switches. Don't use it as a primary database — use it as the read-fast cache in front of one.
"EdgeKV = read-fast KV at every PoP. Use it for flags, tokens, and lookups — not as your source of truth."

quiz.question: Your EdgeWorker needs to check a feature flag. Best store?

options.text: A REST call back to a Linode
options.explain: Adds round-trip latency to every request — defeats the point of edge.

options.text: EdgeKV — read at the edge in single-digit ms
options.explain: EdgeKV is built for exactly this. Update from your control plane; read from any EdgeWorker.

options.text: A cookie on the user's browser
options.explain: You'd be trusting the client. Edge-side flags are server-controlled.
Then every flag flip is a deploy. Defeats the purpose of flags.
It's eventually consistent — writes propagate in seconds.

options.text: Read-optimized, eventually consistent KV at every PoP
options.explain: Reads are fast everywhere. Writes converge globally within seconds. Use accordingly.
Not its job. Pair with a real DB.
Block storage is for filesystems, not KV lookups.
